verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: “I speak after the manner of men because of the infirmity of your flesh: for as ye have yielded your members (so 4 mss.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: Sav. the members of your flesh) servants to uncleanness and to iniquity unto iniquity; even so now yield your members servants to righteousness unto holiness.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: Since he had required great strictness of life, charging us to be dead to the world, and to have died unto wickedness, and to abide with no notion towards the workings of sin, and seemed to be saying something great and burdensome, and too much for human nature; through a desire to show that he is not making any exorbitant demand, nor even as much as might be expected of one who enjoyed so great a gift, but one quite moderate and light, he proves it from contraries, and says, “I speak after the manner of men,” as much as to say, Going by human reasonings; by such as one usually meets with.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: For he signifies either this, or the moderateness of it, by the term applied, “after the manner of men.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: For elsewhere he uses the same word.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: “There hath no temptation taken you but such as is common to man” (1 Cor. x.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: 13), that is, moderate and small.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: “For as ye have yielded your members servants to uncleanness and to iniquity unto iniquity; even so now yield your members servants to righteousness unto holiness.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: And truly the masters are very different ones, but still it is an equal amount of servitude that I ask.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: For men ought to give a much larger one, and so much the larger as this is a greater and better mastership than the other.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: Nevertheless I make no greater demand “because of the infirmity,” and that, he does not say of your free will or readiness of spirit, but “of your flesh,” so making what he says the less severe.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: And yet on one side there is uncleanness, on the other holiness: on the one iniquity, and on the other righteousness.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: And who is so wretched, he says, and in such straits as not to spend as much earnestness upon the service of Christ, as upon that of sin and the devil?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: Hear then what follows, and you will see clearly that we do not even spend this little.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: For when (stated in this naked way) it does not seem credible or easy to admit, and nobody would endure to hear that he does not serve Christ so much as he did serve the devil, he proves it by what follows, and renders it credible by bringing that slavery before us, and saying how they did serve him.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: “For when ye were the servants of sin, ye were free from righteousness.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: Now what he says is somewhat of this kind, When ye lived in wickedness, and impiety, and the worst of evils, the state of compliance ye lived in was such that ye did absolutely no good thing at all.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: For this is, “ye were free from righteousness.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: That is ye were not subject to it, but estranged from it wholly.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: For ye did not even so much as divide the manner of servitude between righteousness and sin, but gave yourselves wholly up to wickedness.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: Now, therefore, since ye have come over to righteousness, give yourselves wholly up to virtue, doing nothing at all of vice, that the measure you give may be at least equal.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: And yet it is not the mastership only that is so different, but in the servitude itself there is a vast difference.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: And this too he unfolds with great perspicuity, and shows what conditions they served upon then, and what now.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: And as yet he says nothing of the harm accruing from the thing, but hitherto speaks of the shame.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: “What fruit had ye then in those things whereof ye are now ashamed?”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: So great was the slavery, that even the recollection of it now makes you ashamed; but if the recollection makes one ashamed, the reality would much more.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: And so you gained now in two ways, in having been freed from the shame; and also in having come to know the condition you were in; just as then ye were injured in two ways, in doing things deserving shame, and in not even knowing to be ashamed.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: And this is worse than the former.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: Yet still ye kept in a state of servitude.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: Having then proved most abundantly the harm of what took place then from the shame of it, he comes to the thing in question.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: Now what is this thing?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: “For the end of those things is death.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: Since then shame seems to be no such serious evil, he comes to what is very fearful, I mean death; though in good truth what he had before mentioned were enough.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: For consider how exceeding great the mischief must be, inasmuch as, even when freed from the vengeance due to it, they could not get free of the shame.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: What wages then, he says, do you expect from the reality, when from the bare recollection, and that too when you are freed from the vengeance, you hide your face and blush, though under such grace as you are!

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: But God’s side is far otherwise.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: “For now being made free from sin, and become servants to God, ye have your fruit unto holiness, and the end everlasting life.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: Of the former, the fruit was shame, even after the being set free.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: Of these the fruit is holiness, and where holiness is, there is all confidence.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: But of those things the end is death, and of these everlasting life.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: Do you see how he points out some things as already given, and some as existing in hope, and from what are given he draws proof of the others also, that is from the holiness of the life.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: For to prevent your saying (i.e. as an objection) everything lies in hope, he points out that you have already reaped fruits, first the being freed from wickedness, and such evils as the very recollection of puts one to shame; second, the being made a servant unto righteousness; a third, the enjoying of holiness; a fourth, the obtaining of life, and life too not for a season, but everlasting.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: Yet with all these, he says, do but serve as ye served it.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: For though the master is far preferable, and the service also has many advantages, and the rewards too for which ye are serving, still I make no further demand.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: Next, since he had mentioned arms and a king, he keeps on with the metaphor in these words:

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: “For the wages of sin is death, but the gift of God is eternal life, through Jesus Christ our Lord.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: After speaking of the wages of sin, in the case of the blessings, he has not kept to the same order (τάξιν, rank or relation): for he does not say, the wages of good deeds, “but the gift of God;” to show, that it was not of themselves that they were freed, nor was it a due they received, neither yet a return, nor a recompense of labors, but by grace all these things came about.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: And so there was a superiority for this cause also, in that He did not free them only, or change their condition for a better, but that He did it without any labor or trouble upon their part: and that He not only freed them, but also gave them much more than before, and that through His Son.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: And the whole of this he has interposed as having discussed the subject of grace, and being on the point of overthrowing the Law next.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: That these things then might not both make them rather listless, he inserted the part about strictness of life, using every opportunity of rousing the hearer to the practice of virtue.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: For when he calls death the wages of sin, he alarms them again, and secures them against dangers to come.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: For the words he uses to remind them of their former estate, he also employs so as to make them thankful, and more secure against any inroads of temptations.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: Here then he brings the hortatory part to a stop, and proceeds with the doctrines again, speaking on this wise.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: Chap. vii. ver.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: “Know ye not, brethren, for I speak to them that know the Law.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: Since then he had said, we are “dead to sin,” he here shows that not sin only, but also the Law, hath no dominion over them.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: But if the Law hath none, much less hath sin: and to render his language palatable, he uses a human example to make this plain by.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: And he seems to be stating one point, but he sets down at once two arguments for his proposition.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: One, that when a husband is dead, the woman is no longer subject to her husband, and there is nothing to prevent her becoming the wife of another man: and the other, that in the present case it is not the husband only that is dead but the wife also.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: So that one may enjoy liberty in two ways.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: Now if when the husband is dead, she is freed from his power, when the woman is shown to be dead also, she is much more at liberty.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: For if the one event frees her from his power, much more does the concurrence of both.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: As he is about to proceed then to a proof of these points, he starts with an encomium of the hearers, in these words, “Know ye not, brethren, for I speak to them that know the Law,” that is, I am saying a thing that is quite agreed upon, and clear, and to men too that know all these things accurately,

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: “How that the Law hath dominion over a man as long as he liveth?”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: He does not say, husband or wife, but “man,” which name is common to either creature; “For he that is dead,” he says, “is freed (Gr. justified) from sin.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: The Law then is given for the living, but to the dead it ceaseth to be ordained (or to give commands).

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: Do you observe how he sets forth a twofold freedom?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: Next, after hinting this at the commencement, he carries on what he has to say by way of proof, in the woman’s case, in the following way.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: “For the woman which hath an husband is bound by the Law to her husband, so long as he liveth: but if the husband be dead, she is loosed from the Law of her husband.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: So then, if while her husband liveth, she be married to another man, she is called an adulteress: but if her husband be dead, she is free from that law; so that she is no adulteress, though she be married to another man.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: He keeps continually upon this point, and that with great exactness, since he feels quite sure of the proof grounded on it: and in the husband’s place he puts the Law, but in the woman’s, all believers.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: Then he adds the conclusion in such way, that it does not tally with the premiss; for what the context would require would be, “and so, my brethren, the Law doth not rule over you, for it is dead.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: But he does not say so, but only in the premiss hinted it, and in the inference, afterwards, to prevent what he says being distasteful, he brings the woman in as dead by saying,

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: “Wherefore, my brethren, ye also are become dead to the Law.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: As then the one or the other event gives rise to the same freedom, what is there to prevent his showing favor to the Law without any harm being done to the cause?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: “For the woman which hath an husband is bound by the Law to her husband as long as he liveth.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: What is become now (3 mss. then) of those that speak evil of the Law?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: Let them hear, how even when forced upon it, he does not bereave it of its dignity, but speaks great things of its power; if while it is alive the Jew is bound, and they are to be called adulterers who transgress it, and leave it whiles it is alive.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: But if they let go of it after it has died, this is not to be wondered at.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: For in human affairs no one is found fault with for doing this: “but if the husband be dead, she is loosed from the law of her husband.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: You see how in the example he points out the Law as dead, but in the inference he does not do so.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: So then if it be while her husband liveth, the woman is called an adulteress.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: See how he dwells upon the accusations of those who transgress the Law, while it is yet living.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: But since he had put an end to it, he afterwards favors it with perfect security, without doing any harm hereby to the faith.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: “For if while her husband liveth, she be married to another man, she is called an adulteress.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: Thus it would have been natural to say next, ye also, my brethren, now the Law is dead, will not be judged guilty of adultery, if ye become married to another husband.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: Yet he does not use these words, but what?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: “Ye are become dead to the Law;” if ye have been made dead, ye are no longer under the Law.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: For if, when the husband is dead, the woman is no longer liable to it, much more when herself is dead also she is freed from the former.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: Do you note the wisdom of Paul, how he points out that the Law itself designs that we should be divorced from it, and married to another?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: For there is nothing, he means, against your living with another husband, now the former is dead; for how should there be, since when the husband was alive it allowed this to her who had a writing of divorcement?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: But this he does not set down, as it was rather a charge against the woman; for although this had been granted, still it was not cleared of blame.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: For in cases where he has gained the victory by requisite and accredited proofs, he does not go into questions beyond the purpose; not being captious.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: The marvel then is this, that it is the Law itself that acquits us who are divorced from it of any charge, and so the mind of it was that we should become Christ’s.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: For it is dead itself, and we are dead; and the grounds of its power over us are removed in a twofold way.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: But he is not content with this alone, but also adds the reason of it.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: For he has not set down death without special purpose, but brings the cross in again, which had wrought these things, and in this way too he puts us under an engagement.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: For ye have not been freed merely, he means, but it was through the Lord’s death.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: For he says,

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: “Ye are become dead to the Law by the Body of Christ.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: Now it is not on this only he grounds his exhortation, but also on the superiority of this second husband.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: And so he proceeds: “that ye should be married to another, even to Him Who is raised from the dead.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: Then to prevent their saying, If we do not choose to live with another husband, what then?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: For the Law does not indeed make an adulteress of the widow who lives in a second marriage, but for all that it does not force her to live in it.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: Now that they may not say this, he shows that from benefits already conferred, it is binding on us to choose it: and this he lays down more clearly in other passages, where he says, “Ye are not your own;” and, “Ye are bought with a price;” and, “Be not ye the servants of men” (1 Cor. vi.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: 23); and again, “One died for all, that they which live should not henceforth live unto themselves, but unto Him which died for them.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: This is then what he here alludes to in the words, “By the Body.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: And next he exhorts to better hopes, saying, “That we should bring forth fruit unto God.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: For then, he means, ye brought forth fruit unto death, but now unto God.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: “For when we were in the flesh, the motions of sins, which were by the Law, did work in our members to bring forth fruit unto death.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: You see then the gain to be got from the former husband!

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: And he does not say when we were in the Law, so in every passage shrinking from giving a handle to heretics; but “when we were in the flesh,” that is, in evil deeds, in a carnal life.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: What he says then is, not that they were in the flesh before, but now they went about without any bodies; but by saying what he does, he neither says that the Law is the cause of sins, nor yet frees it from odium.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: For it held the rank of a bitter accuser, by making their sins bare: since that, which enjoins more to him who is not minded to obey at all, makes the offence greater.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: And this is why he does not say, the “motions of sins” which were produced by the Law, but which “were through the Law” (Rom. ii.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: 27), without adding any “produced,” but simply “through the Law,” that is to say, which through the Law were made apparent, were made known.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: Next that he might not accuse the flesh either; he does not say which the members wrought, but “which did work (or were wrought) in our members,” to show that the origin of the mischief was elsewhere, from the thoughts which wrought in us, not from the members which had them working in them.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: For the soul ranks as a performer, and the fabric of the flesh as a lyre, sounding as the performer obliges it.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: So the discordant tune is to be ascribed not to the latter, but to the former sooner than to the latter.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: “But now,” he says, “we are delivered from the Law.” (κατηργήθημεν, “made of no effect.”)

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: See how he again in this place spares the flesh and the Law.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: For he does not say that the Law was made of no effect, or that the flesh was made of no effect, but that we were made of no effect (i.e., were delivered).

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: And how were we delivered?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: Why by the old man, who was held down by sin, being dead and buried.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: For this is what he sets forth in the words, “being dead to that, wherein we were held.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: As if he had said, the chain by which we were held down was deadened and broken through, so that that which held down, namely sin, held down no more.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: But do not fall back or grow listless.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: For you have been freed with a view to being servants again, though not in the same way, but “in newness of spirit, and not in the oldness of the letter.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: Now what does he mean here? for it is necessary to disclose it here, that when we come upon the passage, we may not be perplexed with it.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: When then Adam sinned (he means), and his body became liable to death and sufferings, it received also many physical losses, and the horse became less active and less obedient.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: But Christ, when He came, made it more nimble for us through baptism, rousing it with the wing of the Spirit.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: And for this reason the marks for the race, which they of old time had to run, are not the same as ours.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: Since then the race was not so easy as it is now.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: For this reason, He desires them to be clear not from murder only, as He did them of old time, but from anger also; nor is it adultery only that He bids them keep clear of, but even the unchaste look; and to be exempt not from false swearing only, but even from true.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: And with their friends He orders them to love their enemies also.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: And in all other duties, He gives us a longer ground to run over, and if we do but obey, threatens us with hell, so showing that the things in question are not matters of free-will offering for the combatants, as celibacy and poverty are, but are binding upon us absolutely to fulfil.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: For they belong to necessary and urgent requisites, and the man who does not do them is to be punished to the utmost.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: This is why He said, “Except your righteousness exceed the righteousness of the Scribes and Pharisees, ye shall in no case enter into the kingdom of heaven.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: But he that does not see the kingdom, shall certainly fall into hell.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: For this cause Paul too says, “Sin shall not have dominion over you, because ye are not under the Law, but under grace.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: And here again, “that ye should serve in newness of spirit, and not in the oldness of the letter.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: For it is not the letter that condemneth, that is the old Law, but the Spirit that helpeth.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: And for this reason among the ancients, if any were found practising virginity, it was quite astonishing.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: But now the thing is scattered over every part of the world.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: And death in those times some few men did with difficulty despise, but now in villages and cities there are hosts of martyrs without number, consisting not of men only, but even of women.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: And next having done with this, he again meets an objection which is rising, and as he meets it, gives confirmation to his own object.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: And so he does not introduce the solution of it as main argument, but by way of opposing this; that by the exigency of meeting it, he may get a plea for saying what he wishes, and make his accusation not so unpalatable.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: Having then said, “in the newness of the Spirit, and not in the oldness of the letter,” he proceeds.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: “What then? is the Law sin?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: God forbid.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: Even before this he had been saying, that “the motions of sins, which were by the Law did work in our members” (ver.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: 5): and, “sin shall have no dominion over you, for ye are not under the Law.” (vi.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: And that “where no law is, there is no transgression.” (iv.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: And, “but the Law came in, that the offence might abound” (v.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: 20); and, “the Law worketh wrath.” (iv.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: Now as all these things seem to bring the Law into disrepute, in order to correct the suspicion arising from them, he supposes also an objection, and says, “What then, is the Law sin?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: God forbid.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: Before the proof he uses this adjuration to conciliate the hearer, and by way of soothing any who was troubled at it.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: For so, when he had heard this, and felt assured of the speaker’s disposition, he would join with him in investigating the seeming perplexity, and feel no suspicions of him.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: Wherefore he has put the objection, associating the other with him.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: Hence, he does not say, What am I to say? but “What shall we say then?”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: As though a deliberation and a judgment were before them, and a general meeting called together, and the objection came forward not of himself, but in the course of discussion, and from real circumstances of the case.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: For that the letter killeth, he means, no one will deny, or that the Spirit giveth life (2 Cor. iii.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: 6); this is plain too, and nobody will dispute it.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: If then these are confessedly truths, what are we to say about the Law? that “it is sin?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: God forbid.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: Explain the difficulty then.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: Do you see how he supposes the opponent to be present, and having assumed the dignity of the teacher, he comes to the explaining of it.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: Now what is this?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: Sin, he says, the Law is not.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: “Nay, I had not known sin, but by the Law.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: Notice the reach of his wisdom!

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: What the Law is not, he has set down by way of objection, so that by removing this, and thereby doing the Jew a pleasure, he may persuade him to accept the less alternative.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: And what is this?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: Why that “I had not known sin, but by the Law.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: For I had not known lust, except the Law had said, Thou shalt not covet.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: Do you observe, how by degrees he shows it to be not an accuser of sin only, but in a measure its producer?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: Yet not from any fault of its own, but from that of the froward Jews, he proves it was, that this happened.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: For he has taken good heed to stop the mouths of the Manichees, that accuse the Law; and so after saying, “Nay, I had not known sin, but by the Law;” and, “I had not known lust, except the Law had said, Thou shalt not covet;” he adds,

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: “But sin, taking occasion by the commandment, wrought in me all manner of concupiscence.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: Do you see how he has cleared it of all blame?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: For “sin,” he says, “taking occasion by the commandment,” it was, and not the Law, that increased the concupiscence, and the reverse of the Law’s intent was brought about.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: This came of weakness, and not of any badness.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: For when we desire a thing, and then are hindered of it, the flame of the desire is but increased.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: Now this came not of the Law; for it hindered us (3 mss. endeavored) of itself to keep us off from it; but sin, that is, thy own listlessness and bad disposition, used what was good for the reverse.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: But this is no fault in the physician, but in the patient who applies the medicine wrongly.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: For the reason of the Law being given was, not to inflame concupiscence, but to extinguish it, though the reverse came of it.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: Yet the blame attaches not to it, but to us.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: Since if a person had a fever, and wanted to take cold drink when it was not good for him, and one were not to let him take his fill of it, and so increase his lust after this ruinous pleasure, one could not deservedly be found fault with.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: For the physician’s business is simply prohibiting it, but the restraining himself is the patient’s.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: And what if sin did take occasion from it?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: Surely there are many bad men who by good precepts grow in their own wickedness.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: For this was the way in which the devil ruined Judas, by plunging him into avarice, and making him steal what belonged to the poor.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: However it was not the being entrusted with the bag that brought this to pass, but the wickedness of his own spirit.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: And Eve, by bringing Adam to eat from the tree, threw him out of Paradise.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: But neither in that case was the tree the cause, even if it was through it that the occasion took place.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: But if he treats the discussion about the Law with somewhat of vehemence, do not feel surprise.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: For Paul is making a stand against the present exigency, and suffers not his language to give a handle even to those that suspected otherwise, but takes great pains to make the present statement correct.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: Do not then sift what he is now going on to say (4 mss. “here saying”) by itself, but put beside it the purpose by which he is led on to speak of these things, and reckon for the madness of the Jews, and their vigorous spirit of contention, which as he desires earnestly to do away with, he seems to bear violently (πολὺς πνεἵν) against the Law, not to find fault with it, but to unnerve their vigor.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: For if it is any reproach to the Law that sin taketh occasion by it, this will be found to be the case in the New Testament also.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: For in the New Testament there are thousands of laws, and about many more (“far more,” Field) important matters.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: And one may see the same come to pass there also, not with regard to covetousness (lust, as v.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: 7) only, but to all wickedness generally.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: For He says, “if I had not come and spoken unto them, they had not had sin,” (John xv.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: Here then sin finds a footing in this fact, and so the greater punishment.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: And again when Paul discourseth about grace, he says, “Of how much sorer punishment, suppose ye, shall he be counted worthy, who hath trodden under foot the Son of God.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: Has not then the worse punishment its origin from hence, from the greater benefit?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: And the reason why he says the Greeks were without excuse was, because being honored with the gift of reason, and having gotten a knowledge of the beauty of the creation, and having been placed in a fair way for being led by it to the Creator, they did not so use the wisdom of God, as it was their duty.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: Seest thou that to the wicked in all cases occasions of greater punishment result from good things?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: But we shall not in this accuse the benefits of God, but rather upon this even admire them the more: but we shall throw the blame on the spirit of those who abuse the blessings to contrary purpose.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: Let this then be our line with regard to the Law also.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: But this is easy and feasible—the other is what is a difficulty.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: How is it that he says “I had not known lust except the Law had said, Thou shalt not covet?”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: Now if man had not known lust, before he received the Law, what was the reason for the flood, or the burning of Sodom?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: What does he mean then?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: He means vehement lust: and this is why he did not say, lust, but “all manner of concupiscence,” intimating, in that, its vehemency.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: And what, it will be said, is the good of the Law, if it adds to the disorder?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: None; but much mischief even.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: Yet the charge is not against the Law, but the listlessness of those who received it.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: For sin wrought it, though by the Law.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: But this was not the purpose of the Law, nay, the very opposite, Sin then became stronger, he says, and violent.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: But this again is no charge against the Law but against their obstinacy.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: “For without the Law sin is dead.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: That is, was not so ascertainable.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: For even those before the Law knew that they had sinned, but they came to a more exact knowledge of it after the giving of the Law.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: And for this reason they were liable to a greater accusation: since it was not the same thing to have nature to accuse them, and besides nature the Law, which told them distinctly every charge.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: “For I was alive without the Law once.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: When, pray, was that?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: Before Moses.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: See how he sets himself to show that it, both by the things it did, and the things it did not do, weighed down human nature.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: For when “I was alive without the Law,” he means, I was not so much condemned.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: “But when the commandment came, sin revived, and I died.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: This seems indeed to be an accusing of theLaw.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: But if any one will look closely at it, it will be seen to be even an encomium of it.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: For it did not give existence to sin that before was not, but only pointed out what had escaped notice.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: And this is even a praise of the Law, if at least before it they had been sinning without perceiving it.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: But when this came, if they gained nothing besides from it, at all events this they were distinctly made acquainted with, the fact that they had been sinning.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: And this is no small point, with a view to getting free from wickedness.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: Now if they did not get free, this has nothing to do with the Law; which framed everything with a view to this end, but the accusation lies wholly against their spirit, which was perverse beyond all supposition.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: For what took place was not the natural thing,—their being injured by things profitable.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: And this is why he says “And the commandment, which was ordained to life, I found to be unto death.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: He does not say, “it was made,” or “it brought forth” death, but “was found,” so explaining the novel and unusual kind of discrepancy, and making the whole fall upon their own pate.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: For if, he says, you would know the aim of it, it led to life, and was given with this view.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: But if death was the issue of this, the fault is with them that received the commandment, and not of this, which was leading them to life.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: And this is a point on which he has thrown fresh light by what follows.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: “For sin taking occasion by the commandment deceived me, and by it slew me.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: You observe how he everywhere keeps to sin, and entirely clears the Law of accusation.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: And so he proceeds as follows.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: “Wherefore the law is holy, and the commandment holy, and just, and good.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: But, if ye be so minded, we will bring before you the language of those who wrest these declarations.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: For this will make our own statements clearer.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: For there are some that say, that he is not here saying what he does of the Law of Moses, but some take it of the law of nature; some, of the commandment given in Paradise.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: Yet surely Paul’s object everywhere is to annul this Law, but he has not any question with those.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: And with much reason; for it was through a fear and a horror of this that the Jews obstinately opposed grace.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: But it does not appear that he has ever called the commandment in Paradise “Law” at all; no, nor yet any other writer.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: Now to make this plainer from what he has really said, let us follow out his words, retracing the argument a little.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: Having then spoken to them about strictness of conversation, he goes on to say, “Know ye not, brethren, how that the Law hath dominion over a man as long as he liveth?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: Wherefore ye are become dead to the Law.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: Therefore if these things are said about the natural law, we are found to be without the natural law.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: And if this be true, we are more senseless than the creatures which are without reason.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: Yet this is not so, certainly.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: For with regard to the law in Paradise, there is no need to be contentious, lest we should be taking up a superfluous trouble, by entering the lists against things men have made up their minds upon.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: In what sense then does he say, “I should not have known sin but by the Law?”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: He is speaking, not of absolute want of knowledge, but of the more accurate knowledge.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: For if this were said of the law of nature, how would what follows suit?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: “For I was alive,” he says, “without the Law once.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: Now neither Adam, nor any body else, can be shown ever to have lived without the law of nature.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: For as soon as God formed him, He put into him that law of nature, making it to dwell by him as a security to the whole kind (Gr.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: Nature, see p.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: And besides this, it does not appear that he has anywhere called the law of nature a commandment.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: But this he calls as well a commandment, and that “just and holy,” as a “spiritual law.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: But the law of nature was not given to us by the Spirit.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: For barbarians, as well as Greeks and other men, have this law.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: Hence it is plain, that it is the Mosaic Law that he is speaking of above, as well as afterwards, and in all the passages.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: For this cause also he calls it holy, saying, “Wherefore the Law is holy, and the commandment holy, and just, and good.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: For even though the Jews have been unclean since the Law, and unjust and covetous, this does not destroy the virtue of the Law, even as their unbelief doth not make the faith of God of none effect.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: So from all these things it is plain, that it is of the Law of Moses that he here speaks.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: “Was then that which is good made death unto me?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: But sin that it might appear sin.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: (4 mss. om. ἡ.)

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: That is, that it might be shown what great evil sin is, namely, a listless will, an inclinableness to the worse side, the actual doing (3 mss. om. this clause), and the perverted judgment.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: For this is the cause of all the evils; but he amplifies it by pointing out the exceeding grace of Christ, and teaching them what an evil He freed the human race from, which, by the medicines used to cure it, had become worse, and was increased by the preventives.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: Wherefore he goes on to say: “That sin, by the commandment, might become exceeding sinful.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: Do you see how these things are woven together everywhere?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: By the very means he uses to accuse sin, he again shows the excellency of the Law.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: Neither is it a small point which he has gained by showing what an evil sin is, and unfolding the whole of its poison, and bringing it to view.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: For this is what he shows, by saying, “that sin by the commandment might become exceeding sinful.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: That is, that it may be made clear what an evil sin is, what a ruinous thing.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: And this is what was shown by the commandment.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: Hereby he also shows the preëminence of grace above the Law, the preëminence above, not the conflict with, the Law.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: For do not look to this fact, that those who received it were the worse for it; but consider the other, that the Law had not only no design of drawing wickedness out to greater lengths, but even seriously aimed at hewing down what already existed.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: But if it had no strength, give to it indeed a crown for its intention, but adore more highly the power of Christ, which abolished, cut away: and plucked up the very roots an evil so manifold and so hard to be overthrown.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: But when you hear me speak of sin, do not think of it as a substantial power, but evil doing, as it comes upon men and goes from them continually, and which, before it takes place, has no being, and when it has taken place, vanishes again.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: This then was why the Law was given.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: Now no law is ever given to put an end to things natural, but in order to correct a way of acting purposely wicked.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: And this the lawgivers that are without too are aware of, and all mankind in general.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: For it is the evils from viciousness alone that they are for setting right, and they do not undertake to extirpate those allotted us along with our nature; since this they cannot do.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: For things natural remain unalterable (Arist.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: 1), as we have told you frequently in other discourses also.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: And so let us leave these contests, and again practise ourselves in exhortation.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: Or rather, this last part belongs to those contests.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: For if we cast out wickedness, we should bring virtue in also: and by these means we shall clearly teach that wickedness is no natural evil, and shall be able easily to stop the mouths of them that enquire for the origin of evil, not by means of words only, but of actions also, since we share the same nature with them, but are freed from their wickedness.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: For let us not be looking at the laboriousness of virtue, but at the possibility of succeeding in it.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: But if we be in earnest, it will be at once light and palatable to us.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: But if you tell me of the pleasure of vice, tell out its end too.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: For it issueth in death, even as virtue leadeth us to life.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: Or if you think fit let us rather scrutinize them both even before their end; for we shall see that vice has a great deal of pain attached to it, and virtue great pleasure.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: For what pray is so painful as a bad conscience? or what more pleasing than a good hope?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: For there is nothing, assuredly there is nothing, which is used to cut us so deep, and press so hard on us, as the expectation of evil: nothing that so keeps us up, and all but gives us wings, as a good conscience.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: And this we may get a knowledge of even by what takes place before our eyes.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: For they that dwell in a prison, and are in expectation of sentence against them, let them have the enjoyment of luxury repeated beyond count, live a more afflicting life than those that go a begging by the by-roads, yet with nothing upon their consciences to trouble them.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: For the expectation of a dreadful end will not let them perceive those pleasures which they have in their hands.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: And why do I speak of prisoners?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: Why, as for those that are living out of prison, and have a good fortune, yet have a bad conscience about them, handicraftsmen that work for their bread, and spend the whole day amid their labor, are in a far better plight than they.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: And for this reason too we say, How miserable the gladiators are (though seeing them as we do in taverns, drunken, luxurious, gormandizing), and call them the most miserable of men, because the calamity of the end which they must expect is too great to admit of comparison with that pleasure.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: Now if to them a life of this sort seems to be pleasing, remember what I am continually telling you, that it is no such marvel that a man who lives in vice should not flee from the misery and pain of vice.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: For see how a thing so detestable as that, yet seems to be delectable to those who practice it.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: Yet we do not on this account say, how happy they are, for this is just the very reason why we think them pitiable, because they have no notion of the evils they are amongst.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: And what would you say of adulterers, who for a little pleasure undergo at once a disgraceful slavery, and a loss of money, and a perpetual fear (Hor.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: 58–67), and in fact the very life of a Cain, or rather one that is even much worse than his; filled with fears for the present, and trembling for the future, and suspecting alike friend and foe, and those that know about it, and those that know nothing?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: Neither when they go to sleep are they quit of this struggle, their bad conscience shaping out for them dreams that abound with sundry terrors, and in this way horrifying them.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: Far otherwise is the chaste man, seeing he passes the present life unshackled and at full liberty.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: Weigh then against the little pleasure, the sundry fluctuations of these terrors, and with the short labor of continency, the calm of an entire life; and you will find the latter hath more of pleasantness than the former.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: But as for the man that is set upon plundering and laying hands upon other men’s goods, tell me if he has not to undergo countless pains in the way of running about, fawning upon slaves, freemen, doorkeepers; alarming and threatening, acting shamelessly, watching, trembling, in agony, suspecting everything.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: Far otherwise is the man that holds riches in contempt, for he too enjoys pleasure in abundance, and lives with no fear, and in perfect security.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: And if any one were to go through the other instances of vice, he would find much trouble, and many rocks.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: But what is of greater importance is, that in the case of virtue the difficulties come first, and the pleasant part afterwards, so the trouble is even thus alleviated.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: But in the case of vice, the reverse.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: After the pleasure, the pains and the punishments, so that by these besides the pleasure is done away.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: For as he who waits for the crown, perceives nothing of present annoyance, so he that has to expect the punishments after the pleasures has no power of gathering in a gladness that is unalloyed, since the fear puts everything in confusion.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: Or rather if any one were to scrutinize the thing with care, even before the punishment which follows upon these things, he would find that even at the very moment when vice is boldly entered upon, a great deal of pain is felt.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: And, if you think fit, let us just examine this in the case of those who plunder other men’s goods.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: Or those who in any way get together money, and setting aside the fears, and dangers, and trembling, and agony, and care, and all these things, let us suppose the case of a man, who has got rich without any annoyance, and feels sure about maintaining his present fortune (which he has no means of doing, still for all that let it be assumed for argument’s sake).

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: What sort of pleasure then is he to gather in from having so much about him?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: On the contrary, it is just this very thing that will not let him be glad-hearted.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: For as long as ever he desires other things besides, he is still upon the rack.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: Because desire gives pleasure at the time it has come to a stand.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: If thirsty, for instance, we feel refreshed, when we have drunk as much as we wish; but so long as we keep thirsty, even if we were to have exhausted all the fountains in the world, our torment were but growing greater; even if we were to drink up ten thousand rivers, our state of punishment were more distressing.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: And thou also, if thou wert to receive the goods of the whole world, and still to covet, wouldest make thy punishment the greater, the more things thou hadst tasted of.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: Fancy not then, that from having gathered a great sum together thou shalt have aught of pleasure, but rather by declining to be rich.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: But if thou covetest to be rich thou wilt be always under the scourge.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: For this is a kind of love that does not reach its aim; and the longer journey thou hast gone, the further off thou keepest from the end.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: Is not this a paradox then, a derangement, a madness in the extreme?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: Let us then forsake this first of evils, or rather let us not even touch this covetousness at all.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: Yet, if we have touched it, let us spring away from its first motions (προοιμίων).

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: For this is the advice the writer of the Proverbs gives us, when he speaks about the harlot: “Spring away,” he says, “tarry not, neither go thou near to the door of her house” (Prov. v.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: 8): this same thing I would say to you about the love of money.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: For if by entering gradually you fall into this ocean of madness, you will not be able to get up out of it with ease, and as if you were in whirlpools, struggle as often as ever you may, it will not be easy for you to get clear; so after falling into this far worse abyss of covetousness, you will destroy your own self, with all that belongs to you.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: And so my advice is that we be on our watch against the beginning, and avoid little evils, for the great ones are gendered by these.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: For he who gets into a way of saying at every sin, This matters nothing! will by little and little ruin himself entirely.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: At all events it is this which has introduced vice; which has opened the doors to the robber (5 mss. devil), which has thrown down the walls of cities, this saying at each sin, “This matters nothing!”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: Thus in the case of the body too, the greatest of diseases grow up, when trifling ones are made light of.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: If Esau had not first been a traitor to his birthright, he would not have become unworthy of the blessings.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: If he had not rendered himself unworthy of the blessings, he would not have had the desire of going on to fratricide.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: If Cain had not fallen in love with the first place, but had left that to God, he would not have had the second place.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: Again, when he had the second place, if he had listened to the advice, he would not have travailed with the murder.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: Again, if after doing the murder he had come to repentance, when God called him, and had not answered in an irreverent way, he would not have had to suffer the subsequent evils.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: But if those before the Law did owing to this listlessness come to the very bottom of misery, only consider what is to become of us, who are called to a greater contest, unless we take strict heed unto ourselves, and make speed to quench the sparks of evil deeds before the whole pile is kindled.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: Take an instance of my meaning.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: Are you in the habit of false swearing? do not stop at this only, but away with all swearing, and you will have no further need of trouble.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: For it is far harder for a man that swears to keep from false swearing, than to abstain from swearing altogether.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: Are you an insulting and abusive person? a striker too?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: Lay down as a law for yourself not to be angry or brawl in the least, and with the root the fruit also will be gotten rid of.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: Are you lustful and dissipated?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: Make it your rule again not even to look at a woman (Job xxxi.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: 1), or to go up into the theatre, or to trouble yourself with the beauty of other people whom you see about.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: For it is far easier not even to look at a woman of good figure, than after looking and taking in the lust, to thrust out the perturbation that comes thereof, the struggle being easier in the preliminaries (προοιμίοις).

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: Or rather we have no need of a struggle at all if we do not throw the gates open to the enemy, or take in the seeds of mischief (κακίας).

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
verses.text: And this is why Christ chastised the man who looks unchastely upon a woman (Matt. v.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on Paul's Epistle to the Romans
